audit_scope: all background premises, method operations, and scene scientific claims

background_items.source_excerpt:
method or a graphical type curve method. The behavior of unconﬁned ﬂow differs signiﬁcantly from that
                                           of conﬁned ﬂow if pumping continues long enough. The temporal distribution of measured drawdown data
                                           often demonstrates an S‐shaped curve, which may be divided into three segments as early time (ﬁrst seg-
                                           ment), intermediate time (second segment), and late time periods (third segment; Neuman, 1972). The
                                           pumped water is mainly from the aquifer storage in the ﬁrst segment. Later, the effect of the gravity drainage
                                           due to the water table decline becomes profound and the temporal drawdown curve exhibits a ﬂattened seg-
                                           ment in the second segment. In the third segment, the pumped water is from both the aquifer storage and the
                                           pore space on the water table.

After the pore water drained above the water table, the curvature of the air‐water interface within the soil
             particles becomes sharper with increasing capillary suction. Large pores in the unsaturated zone may empty
             under lower suction, while small pores with the interfaces (menisci) of sharper curvature would empty at
             higher suction (Bear, 1979, p. 198). A certain amount of water in the pore is retained by the capillary force
             against the gravitational force. It takes time to complete the drainage, and the time lag should be taken into
             account (Bear, 1979, p. 206). Nwankwor et al. (1992) stated that when the rate of the water table declining is

That causes a delayed‐response region (i.e., the second segment of the unconﬁned temporal drawdown
             curve) of the time‐drawdown curve, indicating that the pore water in the capillary fringe takes time to com-
             plete the drainage. Traditional unconﬁned ﬂow models (e.g., Neuman, 1972, 1974) of assuming instanta-
             neous and complete drainage at the water table may sometimes fail to give an accurate estimate of
             speciﬁc yield (Endres et al., 2007; Malama, 2011; Nwankwor et al., 1992).

method_items.source_locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Model Description, Equation 1
method_items.source_excerpt:
2. Methodology
                                     Figure 1 shows the schematic diagram for the conceptual model describing spatiotemporal head distribution
                                     due to a CRP test with a constant rate Q [L3/T] in an unconﬁned aquifer. The drawdown s [L] is used in the
                                     following sections to represent a reduction in the hydraulic head measured at an observation point. The ori-
                                     gin of the coordinate (r, z) [L] is set at the center of the well and the bottom of the aquifer. The averaged aqui-
                                     fer thickness is b [L]. The pumping well has a ﬁnite radius rw [L] with a casing radius rc [L] and partially
                                     penetrates the aquifer with a screen installed from elevation z = d to z = l [L]. The aquifer is anisotropic with
                                     vertical hydraulic conductivity Kz and radial hydraulic conductivity Kr [L/T]. The water extracted by the well
                                     is produced from the aquifer storage and the gravity drainage on the water table. The aquifer speciﬁc storage
                                     and speciﬁc yield are respectively denoted as Ss [1/L] and Sy [‐].
                                     2.1. Model Description

where τq and τs denote the lag times [T] of the ﬂux and aquifer drawdown, respectively. The parameter τq is
             considered to reﬂect the pore water quickly released from the capillary fringe induced by the increase of the
             vertical hydraulic gradient due to pumping. On the other hand, the τs is introduced to reﬂect the lag time for
             drainage from excess storage of water held by the capillary suction.

method_items.source_locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.1 Model Description, Equation 9
method_items.source_excerpt:
4. Concluding Remarks
             This study proposes a new analytical model for describing the spatiotemporal drawdown distribution
             induced by a CRP test in an unconﬁned aquifer considering the lagging effect proposed by Lin and Yeh
             (2017) on the water table decline. Two lag times (i.e., τq and τs) are introduced to Darcy's law for developing
             a new linearized free surface equation describing the water table decline. The model incorporates the well-
             bore storage effect in the inner boundary condition describing the ﬂux across the wellbore. The drawdown
             solution is developed by the use of the Laplace transform and Weber transform. With the present solution,
             the sensitivity analysis is carried out to explore the inﬂuence of the changes in aquifer parameters on the
             temporal drawdown distributions. In addition, the present solution is applied to estimate the aquifer para-
             meters via nonlinear least squares method for the CRP tests performed in unconﬁned aquifers at the sites
             of Cape Cod, Massachusetts; Canadian Forces Base Borden, Ontario; and Saint Pardon de Conques,
             Gironde (France). Our new ﬁndings are given below:
             1. The present linearized free surface equation can reduce to that of Neuman (1972) when τs = 0 and τq = 0.
                It can also reduce to that of Moench (1995) when τqD = 0 and τsD = 1/α1D.

The drawdown in the time domain is obtained by numerically inverting equation (18) with the Crump
             (1976) algorithm. The numerical integration in equation (18) can be achieved by the Mathematica function
             NIntegrate (Wolfram Research, Inc, 2018).

method_items.source_excerpt:
3.2. Sensitivity Analysis
                                                                      Sensitivity analysis is a useful tool in investigating the effect of parameter
                                                                      uncertainty on the aquifer drawdown. One may deﬁne the normalized
                                                                      sensitivity as (Liou & Yeh, 1997):
                                                                                                                  ∂s
                                                                                                      X k ¼ Pk                                 (25)
                                                                                                                 ∂Pk
                                                                      where Xk is the normalized sensitivity of drawdown s to the kth input
                                                                      parameter Pk. A forward ﬁnite difference formula is used to approximate
                                                                      the ﬁrst derivative term in equation (25) written as
                                                                                                         sðPk þ ΔPk Þ−sðPk Þ
                                                                                              X k ¼ Pk                                         (26)
                                                                                                                ΔPk
                                                                      where ΔPk is set to 10−3 Pk (Yeh & Han, 1989).

method_items.source_locator: PDF pages 7-9, Section 3.3 Pumping Test Data Analysis: Cape Cod Site, Table 1 and Figure 4
method_items.source_excerpt:
Four sets of drawdown data obtained from a CRP test conducted by the U.
                                                                                S. Geological Survey in glacial outwash deposit at Cape Cod,
                                                                                Massachusetts (Moench et al., 2001) are analyzed. The aquifer consists
                                                                                of sand and gravel with a saturated thickness of 51.82 m and overlies a
                                                                                ﬁne‐grained formation considered as an impermeable layer. The CRP test
                                                                                was conducted at the well F507‐080 for a 72‐hr pumping with a constant
                                                                                rate of 0.0202 m3/s. The well is screened from 4.02 to 18.35 m below the
                                                                                steady water table with 11‐inch diameter (0.28 m) in the screen part and
                                                                                13.5‐inch diameter (0.34 m) in the casing part. To determine the aquifer
                                                                                hydraulic parameters, the data obtained from four observation wells are
                                                                                considered. The distance from the pumping well is 7.28 m to observation
                                                                                well F505‐032, 14.2 m to well F504‐032, 25.9 m to well F377‐037, and
                                                                                68.8 m to well F347‐031. Table 1 lists the aquifer parameters estimated
                                                                                by the ﬁve solutions coupled with the Levenberg Marquardt algorithm
                                                                                given in the Mathematica routine NonlinearModelFit (Wolfram
                                                                                Research, Inc, 2018). The standard error of estimate (SEE) and mean error
                                                                                (ME) deﬁned by Yeh (1987) are used to assess prediction errors. Judging
                                                                                from the values of SEE and ME, the present solution gives a very good
                                                                                ﬁt to the measured drawdown data as compared with the others. The mag-

method_items.source_locator: PDF pages 9-11, Section 3.4 Pumping Test Data Analysis: Borden Site, Table 2 and Figure 5
method_items.source_excerpt:
Figure 5 displays the measured drawdown data and predicted drawdown
                                                                             curves by the present and those four existing solutions with the estimated
                                                                             parameters shown in Table 2. The ﬁgure shows excellent ﬁts for the pre-
                                                                             sent solution to all the measured data except that of WD1A in the early
                                                                             pumping time. The predicted drawdown curves by the Neuman (1974),
                                                                             Tartakovsky and Neuman (2007), and Malama (2011) solutions signiﬁ-
                                                                             cantly deviate from the data of WD1A, WD1B, and WD2A before
                                                                             100 min. Notably, these ﬁve solutions predict very close results for
                                                                             WD4A, indicating the lagging effects may be negligible for a further dis-
                                                                             tance from the pumping well. The drawdown curves predicted by the pre-
                                                                             sent solution agree well to all the data sets except that of WD1A in the
                                                                             early pumping time.

                                                                             Moench (1994) applied the Neuman (1974) solution to ﬁt the same mea-
                                                                             sured data for both observation wells via the type‐curve method. He sta-
                                                                             ted that matching late time data can obtain more accurate estimated
